title: How to Photograph the Moon
excerpt: The Moon is the most accessible target in the sky and endlessly rewarding to shoot. Learn settings for every phase, gear tips, and advanced techniques.
author: Ace Stellar
body:
## Your First Telescope-Free Target

The Moon is the astrophotography subject that's always there for you. You don't need to drive hours to a dark sky site. You don't need a $2000 star tracker. You don't even need to stay up late. A camera with a decent zoom lens and a tripod is all it takes to capture jaw-dropping lunar detail that'll have people asking "did you really take that?"

Yes. Yes you did.

## Why the Moon Is Trickier Than You Think

Here's the problem most beginners face: they point their camera at the Moon using Auto mode, and they get a bright white circle surrounded by black sky. No craters, no detail, just a glowing blob.

This happens because your camera's metering sees all that dark sky and thinks "this scene is dark, I should brighten everything." It overexposes the Moon into oblivion.

The fix? Manual mode and understanding that the Moon is a sunlit object. It's literally reflecting direct sunlight, which makes it as bright as a sunlit landscape on Earth.

## The Looney 11 Rule

This classic rule gives you a starting point:

**At f/11, set your shutter speed to 1/ISO.**

So at ISO 100, f/11, shoot 1/100 second. At ISO 200, shoot 1/200. Simple and surprisingly accurate for a full or gibbous Moon.

For different phases, you'll need to adjust:

- **Full Moon:** Looney 11 works perfectly. f/11, 1/ISO.
- **Gibbous (75%):** Open up about 1 stop. f/11, double the exposure time.
- **Quarter (50%):** Open up 2 stops from Looney 11.
- **Crescent (25% or less):** Open up 3-4 stops. The thin crescent is much dimmer.
- **Earthshine (dark side glow):** This requires dramatically different settings — ISO 800-1600, 1-2 second exposures. You'll overexpose the bright crescent to capture the faint glow on the dark side.

## Gear for Lunar Photography

**Camera:** Any camera with manual controls. Both DSLR and mirrorless work great.

**Lens:** The longer, the better.
- 200mm — Gets a reasonable Moon, great for Moon-in-landscape shots.
- 300-400mm — Fills more of the frame. Good entry point for detail.
- 500-600mm — Excellent detail. The sweet spot for most lunar photographers.
- 800mm+ or telescope — Frame-filling, revealing individual craters and mountains.

**Tip:** A 2x teleconverter doubles your focal length for minimal cost. A 300mm lens becomes 600mm. You lose 2 stops of light, but the Moon is bright enough that this doesn't matter.

**Tripod:** Essential for anything over 300mm. At extreme focal lengths, even mirror slap causes blur, so use mirror lockup or electronic first curtain shutter.

## When to Shoot: The Terminator Is Your Friend

No, not Arnold. The terminator is the line between the illuminated and dark portions of the Moon. It's where the most dramatic detail lives because the low-angle sunlight creates long shadows that reveal craters, mountains, and valleys in spectacular relief.

A full Moon, while impressive to the eye, actually looks flat in photos because the Sun is hitting it straight-on (no shadows). The most detailed, textured Moon photos come from quarter and gibbous phases when the terminator crosses interesting terrain.

**Best phases for detail:**
- First Quarter and Third Quarter (50% illuminated) — Dramatic terminator across the center
- Waxing/Waning Gibbous (60-80%) — Beautiful balance of coverage and shadow detail

## Step-by-Step Moon Shoot

**1. Set up your tripod on stable ground.** Use a remote shutter or 2-second timer.

**2. Find the Moon and center it** in your frame using your longest focal length.

**3. Focus precisely.**
- Switch to manual focus.
- Use Live View and zoom in 5-10x.
- Focus on the edge of a crater or the terminator line until it's razor-sharp.
- Autofocus can actually work on the Moon (it's a high-contrast subject), but verify with manual zoom-in.

**4. Set your exposure** using the Looney 11 rule as a starting point:
- Full Moon: f/11, 1/100s, ISO 100
- Quarter Moon: f/8, 1/100s, ISO 100
- Crescent: f/8, 1/50s, ISO 200

**5. Shoot in bursts.** Take 20-50 frames. Atmospheric turbulence causes the Moon to shimmer and blur frame-to-frame. More frames means more chances of catching a sharp one, and you can stack the best ones.

**6. Try different exposures.** Bracket your shots. Some craters look better with slightly different exposure.

## Capturing Earthshine

Earthshine is the faint glow on the dark side of a crescent Moon, caused by sunlight reflecting off Earth back to the Moon. It's ethereal and beautiful.

To capture it:
- Shoot a thin crescent Moon (2-5 days old or before new Moon)
- Expose for the earthshine: ISO 800-1600, 1-2 seconds, f/5.6-8
- The bright crescent will be overexposed — that's OK
- For a balanced shot, take two exposures (one for the crescent, one for earthshine) and blend them in Photoshop

## Eclipse Photography

**Lunar Eclipse:**
- Totality is MUCH dimmer than a normal Moon. During totality, you may need ISO 800-1600 and 1-2 second exposures.
- Shoot a timelapse from start to finish — one frame every 2-5 minutes, then composite them into a single image showing the progression.
- During partial phases, expose for the bright portion and let the eclipsed part go dark.

**Solar Eclipse (with proper solar filter):**
- Partial phases: Solar filter on, ISO 100, f/8, 1/500-1/1000
- Totality ONLY: Filter OFF (only during total eclipse), ISO 200-800, bracket from 1/1000 to 1 second to capture everything from prominences to the outer corona.
- NEVER remove the solar filter during partial phases. It will destroy your camera sensor and your eyes.

## Stacking for Maximum Detail

Just like planetary imaging, you can dramatically improve lunar sharpness by stacking:

1. Shoot 50-200 frames (or video at 30-60fps for 30-60 seconds).
2. Load into AutoStakkert or PIPP + Registax.
3. The software aligns all frames, selects the sharpest, and averages them.
4. Apply wavelets in Registax for final sharpening.

The improvement is dramatic. A single frame looks good. A stack of the best 50 frames looks like you used a professional observatory.

## Moon + Landscape Compositions

Don't forget that the Moon in a landscape can be just as powerful as a close-up:

- **Moonrise/Moonset over landmarks** — Use a telephoto lens (200-400mm) from far away to compress the Moon against a building, mountain, or tree.
- **Moon with silhouettes** — Expose for the Moon and let the foreground go to silhouette.
- **Moonlit landscapes** — A full Moon provides enough light for landscape photography. ISO 1600, f/2.8, 15-30 seconds creates an eerie, blue-toned landscape.

## Common Moon Photography Mistakes

- **Overexposure** — The #1 mistake. Use manual mode and the Looney 11 rule.
- **Atmospheric distortion** — Shooting the Moon when it's low on the horizon means shooting through more atmosphere. Wait until it's higher (30+ degrees altitude) for the sharpest shots.
- **Camera shake** — At 500mm+, even pressing the shutter button causes blur. Use a remote trigger and mirror lockup.
- **Shooting only full Moons** — Quarter and gibbous phases are far more photogenic.

The Moon changes every single night. Every phase offers something different, every month brings new opportunities. Keep shooting it — you'll never get bored.
